item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I actually had the same thought process when writing it. The original idea only took me as far as society collapsing. Once I'd written that, I thought, hey, that's kind of cynical. So I wrote the last page, then went back and rewrote large swaths of it so that the story flowed more naturally to that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As soon as I read the part talking about it being able to solve NP-hard problems, my first thought was, "Oh shit. That's going to mess up encryption, which is going to cause the collapse of society."

AND THEN YOU HAD THAT HAPPEN. :D

After that, though, I thought, "Well… Hold on. This is kind of cynical. Wouldn't this also end up causing a technological Renaissance?"

AND THEN THAT HAPPENED IN THE LAST PAGE. :D

+1 for many reasons, but largely because you managed to so effectively set up and pay off those plot points.

item.title: Image License
item.content:encoded:
(Putting this under the top comment for visibility)

The SCP Federation logo was created by the author as a modification of the SCP Foundation logo, under the usual CC BY-SA 3.0 license. Feel free to use it in your own work, with attribution! If you write any articles about the Federation I'd love to read them, but you don't need my permission to use the logo in your own work.

In-universe, the logo is a mashup between the Foundation logo and the astrological symbol for the Earth, ⊕. The fourth arrow represents leadership. The fact that it resembles a crosshair is, uh, probably just a coincidence.

item.title: Ah yes, everyday normal SCP
item.content:encoded:
Very cool, love the twist from benign to world-ending. Feels like a D&D player figuring out that a certain spell has enough power to destroy the moon or something.

Also you should probably highlight the change to Keter (EDIT: as well as to Safe) in blue.

+1

item.title: Logo image credit/licensing
item.content:encoded: The SCP Federation logo was created by myself as a modification of the SCP Foundation logo, under the usual CC BY-SA 3.0 license. Feel free to use it in your own work, with attribution! If you write any articles about the Federation I'd love to read them, but you don't need my permission to use the logo.

item.title: oh boy
item.content:encoded: I loved this one! New favourite SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: definitely one of my favorites, good read

My first +100 SCP! I’d like to thank the Academy, my cat, and Professor Erik Demaine, author of the paper I cited, for being a nerd and studying the mathematics of Tetris.

If you like this article, check out my my Author Page for more of my stuff!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! You hit the nail on the head with your thoughts here. In particular, I wanted to explore how the same technology could be used to either destroy or save the world, depending on how and when it was used. Not enough scps have happy endings either, so I knew I wanted to conclude on the "saving the world" note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really went in a place I didn't expect it to. One thing that drew me in about early SCP articles was that it would take mysterious or mystical items and try to understand them in a modern scientific light.

While the Foundation contains thousands of anomalies which could radically alter the "regular" world, most just end up in a locker somewhere. I really liked that this explored what would happen if something like this existed in real life: yeah it would be weird to look at Tetris getting done quick, but the forces of economics would use it to achieve something like this.

Is P = NP? Who knows, but with this anomaly proliferated, it may as well be true: this scip is practically Explained.

I also liked the nod to the Foundation's reconstruction from its claim as a self-appointed leader of all human affairs to being a moderative force for the new interstellar human race. A happy ending with some troubles in between, something the SCPverse doesn't get a whole ton.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's loosely assumed by modern-day AI researchers that simulating intelligence would involve some gnarly NP-hard problems. SCP-4163 wouldn't necessarily be used to directly run machine intelligences, but the algorithms needed to build the hardware and software for AI would be developed by people using SCP-4163. In other words, SCP-4163 lets us lay the groundwork for the development of further hyperadvanced technology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well written, but one question:

At first I thought that even AI will be affected by SCP-4163, but the first iteration mentions the limitation of human being, so it seems unlikely. But if that’s the case, it means that it needs a human to play a modified version of SCP-4163 to solve an NP-hard problem.

If that’s the case, I can accept that simulations that were hard now can be solved by “letting a bunch of players play Tetris”, but how would it “led to the development of sentient machine intelligences”?

Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice to see SCPs based more on the recognition of the importance of systems that are so ingrained that they're overlooked.

item.title: hehe 69
item.content:encoded: This was pretty cool, plus I just had to upvote to get it to +69

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it goes from, Hey, She’s good at Tetris, To I have disassembled modern mathematics via a Russian video game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The foundation almost gets wiped out by tetris

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one even before everything went to shit. Great writing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The response to anyone being to log in anywhere and see any information with just physical access would be "shut it down". So yeah the financial collapse of everything. I'm surprised that no nukes were fired as I am sure someone would have just logged in and fired them already. Damn Tetris :) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did find the consequences of broken encryption to be weirdly large

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belated reply: That is a very good point! I've edited the last page, and I think it does read more smoothly now. Thank you for your feedback :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand the need for strikethrough text on the final page when that's not the "edit" gimmick the rest of the work uses.

Doubly sticks out because it's showing that, for some strange reason, they couldn't be arsed to make minor updates to the file for the anomaly that singlehandedly reshaped their organisation and society at large for damn near a century.

item.title: Author’s (pretentious) statement on art and stuff
item.content:encoded: The craziest and most optimistic part about this post-singularity society is the fusion of the arts and sciences. I think a world where “poetry” and “physics” were studied and appreciated under the same banner would be pretty excellent.

item.title: P=NP???
item.content:encoded: And someone finally found a way to implement the p=np problem into an SCP. Take my upvotes.

item.title: (TETRIS THEME INTENSIFIES)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of that extranormal event. I forget which one. Where the Tetris theme played from a car dealership for 2 hours.

item.title: It's over 4000!!!
item.content:encoded: GENIUS. but SCP- 682 can NOT be a Tetris variant. Just saying. Unless it's a "find an efficient way to kill 682 without killing everyone NP problem

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"For the good of all of us, except the ones who are dead."

I will not see Tetris the same way again. The idea of turning a relatively straightforward game into a civilization-ending anomaly was brilliant. The eventual outcome was completely unexpected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My original thought for this SCP was "the Foundation secretly engineered the disaster so they could take over the world!"

But I realized (after I wrote it out) that idea is kinda boring and not as clever as I thought.

So instead I went with the current concept: Where both the negative and positive ramifications of this SCP are explored, and the Foundation was reacting to it according to their motto: Secure, Contain, Protect. And where things actually end well for the humanoid SCP! Everybody wins. Except all the people who died in the Collapse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, 4000 is a bit egregious. I toned it down.

I originally didn't intend for there to be back-links, in favor of the left-hand box on the bottom referring to the current page. But if that layout is confusing, I might edit it for easier navigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a couple things: on the final revision, the “see previous page revision” option isn’t linked to the previous one.

I think it’s cool how the world experienced a huge technological advancement after “The Collapse.” If anything I feel like some extra information would spice this up- maybe some interview logs and addendums (describing the current state of specific nations in the penultimate revision?).

Anyway I think this is pretty grea-

There are 17 known more than 4000 known sub-anomalies of SCP-4163

Wait a second.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did some thinking about this, and while I think I'm going to stick with the rapid escalation, upon reflection I agree that the ending was unsatisfying. I've written an epilogue and added it as a fourth and final page that I hope is a better conclusion!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a big fan of slow-burn creeping horror fiction, and the original draft of this SCP did have at least one more intermediary page, but I wanted to see how a full-throttle escalation from "huh that's not great" to "everything's on fire" would work in the context of an SCP. I'm happy with the result, but I understand if it's not for everyone! Thank you for your feedback :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. The core idea is clever, but I feel like the escalation from "Tetris" to "oops it's everywhere" to "oops it allows anyone to break all encryption" to "oops the world ended" goes too quickly to be satisfying.

Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written and interesting enough. Tentative +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that escalated quickly. Remember kid, don't cheat at video games!
